name: Long Island Sound (CT)
description:
Long Island Sound is a tidal estuary stretching along Connecticut's entire southern coast, providing some of the best saltwater fishing in the Northeast. The sound's mix of rocky reefs, sandy flats, and river mouths create diverse fish habitat.

Striped bass migrate through the sound from April through November, with fall providing the best trophy fishing. Bluefish blitzes create exciting surface action during summer, while fluke and blackfish offer excellent bottom fishing. Public launches in virtually every coastal town provide convenient access.

name: Connecticut River
description:
The Connecticut River is New England's longest river, flowing 410 miles through four states before emptying into Long Island Sound. The lower Connecticut River in the state is a rich estuary supporting both freshwater and saltwater species.

The spring American shad run is a beloved New England tradition, with fish running from April through June. Striped bass push up the river following the baitfish, providing exciting catch-and-release fishing. Above Hartford, excellent smallmouth and largemouth bass fishing awaits. Multiple state launches provide boat access throughout the river.

name: Candlewood Lake
description:
Candlewood Lake is Connecticut's largest lake, a man-made reservoir created in 1928 by diverting water from the Housatonic River. The lake spans five towns in western Connecticut and offers diverse fishing opportunities.

Largemouth bass are the primary target, with the lake producing fish over 7 pounds regularly. Smallmouth bass provide action along rocky points and drop-offs. Walleye were stocked and have established a reproducing population. Public launch access is limited, so the state boat launch on the east shore is the primary entry point.

name: Lake Lillinonah
description:
Lake Lillinonah is a reservoir on the Housatonic River in western Connecticut, formed by Shepaug Dam. The narrow, winding lake follows the river valley and offers surprisingly good fishing within easy reach of the Danbury and Waterbury metro areas.

Largemouth bass fishing is the primary attraction, with abundant cover in the form of fallen timber, lily pads, and rocky banks. The lake also supports a healthy walleye population that provides good fishing during low-light periods. Two state-maintained boat launches provide access.

name: Bantam Lake
description:
Bantam Lake is the largest natural lake in Connecticut, located in the scenic Litchfield Hills of northwestern Connecticut. The shallow, weedy lake provides classic New England warmwater fishing in a picturesque setting.

Largemouth bass and chain pickerel are the primary game fish, thriving in the extensive weed beds and lily pad fields. The lake is an excellent panfish destination, with crappie and perch providing fast action, especially in spring. A public boat launch on the east shore provides the main access point.

name: Lake Zoar
description:
Lake Zoar is a narrow reservoir on the Housatonic River between Stevenson Dam and Shepaug Dam in southwestern Connecticut. The lake winds through wooded hills and offers a quiet fishing experience despite its proximity to populated areas.

Walleye fishing has improved dramatically thanks to stocking efforts, with fish commonly caught in the 3-5 pound range. Largemouth and smallmouth bass occupy different zones based on bottom structure. Trout are stocked in the upper portions near river inflows. The state boat launch at Kettletown State Park provides the primary access.
